title: Cat Head Pressing: Symptoms, Causes, and Why It Is an Emergency
meta_desc: Head pressing in cats is a critical medical emergency. Learn why your cat is pressing their head against walls and when to see a vet immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Head pressing is a critical medical emergency indicating serious neurological or metabolic distress; seek immediate veterinary care and record a video of the behavior to assist the medical team.

What exactly is head pressing in cats?

Head pressing is a clinical sign where a cat compulsively presses their head against a wall, corner, or other hard object for extended periods. This behavior is distinct from 'head bunting,' the affectionate rubbing cats do against your hand or furniture. True head pressing usually occurs without a social trigger and is often a sign of damage to the nervous system or brain.

Is it a medical emergency if my cat is head pressing?

Urgency Level: High. If you observe your cat pressing their head against a surface, you should treat it as a medical emergency. This behavior often indicates severe underlying issues such as:

  • Toxic poisoning
  • Metabolic disorders (such as liver failure)
  • Brain tumors
  • Infectious diseases

Delaying treatment can lead to irreversible damage or loss of life.

How does taking a video or photo of my cat help with veterinary triage?

  • Visual evidence is invaluable because cats often experience an adrenaline surge at the clinic that can temporarily mask neurological symptoms.
  • A recording allows the veterinary team to see exactly what you observed in a home environment.
  • Providing these files leads to a faster, more accurate triage process and diagnosis.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Head pressing in cats can manifest as compulsive pressing of the head against walls or other objects. Neurologic signs associated with head pressing can include blindness, abnormal mentation, seizures, ataxia, opisthotonos, and teeth grinding. Intracranial diseases, such as pituitary masses, paranasal masses, meningiomas, and lymphosarcoma, can cause head pressing. Additionally, cardiac arrest during anesthesia or severe head trauma can lead to central blindness and head pressing. Head pressing is an emergency, suggestive of severe neurological dysfunction, and requires immediate veterinary attention for diagnosis and treatment.

Chapter: Neurology, Ophthalmology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1700)

faqs.question: Is head pressing the same as rubbing against my legs?
faqs.answer: No. Rubbing is a social behavior used to mark territory or show affection. Head pressing is a persistent, forceful pressure against a surface and is usually accompanied by other signs like lethargy, pacing, or disorientation.

faqs.question: What are the common causes of head pressing in cats?
faqs.answer: The most common causes include prosencephalon disease (brain damage), liver shunts, lead poisoning, strokes, or severe inflammatory infections such as Feline Infectious Peritonitis (FIP).

faqs.question: Are there other symptoms I should look for?
faqs.answer: Yes. Look for dilated pupils, constant pacing, circling in one direction, changes in vision, or a sudden loss of coordination (ataxia).
